clinical_history: What is the mechanism of the apparent beat-to-beat (2:1) QRS alternation here?
diagnosis: The rhythm is sinus with ventricular bigeminy and late diastolic premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) that come just after the "second" sinus P waves. The PVCs have a right bundle branch block (RBBB)/right axis morphology, but this is not intermittent RBBB per se or Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) pattern, which the present pattern simulates. Nor is this alternation-type pattern due to pericardial effusion/tamponade, where there is usually sinus tachycardia with 2:1 alternation of narrow (normal) or wide QRS complexes, and often low or relatively low voltage QRS complexes.  The normally conducted beats here show borderline left axis deviation with a normal QRS duration (possible left anterior fascicular block) and non-specific ST-T changes. This example shows how late diastolic PVCs in a bigeminal sequence can simulate a number of alternations-type patterns and delineates the differential diagnosis of some of the alternations patterns. Can you think of others? (e.g., QRS alternation with PSVT rhythms). Note also that the apparent "retrograde" P waves in some leads (e.g., V3) are actually within the QRS complex and are due to the RBBB. The actual sinus P waves come on time, with every other one followed immediately by a PVC.
